request.question: Who wrote the debut song on the debut album of the artist who also released the album "I'm Still Here"?
request.answer: Billy Henderson and Steven Dale Jones

title: Neon Hitch discography
The Love Song (Jeff Bates song)
Crazy Over You
A Little More Country Than That
Mindy McCready
You Won't Ever Be Lonely (song)
Ten Thousand Angels (song)
Drunker Than Me
A Better Man
What I Really Meant to Say
sentences: The discography of British singer and songwriter Neon Hitch.
Neon's debut digital single "Get Over U" was released in February 2011.
Hitch then released her single "Bad Dog", which was intended to be the lead single from her debut album.
Later in 2011, she was featured on Gym Class Heroes' song "Ass Back Home".
In 2012, Neon Hitch released "Fuck U Betta" and Gold" featuring Tyga, her official first and second singles respectively.
Both songs peaked at number one on the "Billboard" Dance/Club Play chart.
Neon then debuted an EP entitled "Happy Neon" in January 2013, which was released online for free.
In October 2013, Neon announced that her debut album "Beg, Borrow & Steal" had been scrapped and she would release a new album that had more of her soul in it.
In January 2014, Neon released the mixtape "301 to Paradise" for free.

In May 2014, it was announced that Hitch had parted ways with her label Warner Bros. and was gearing up to releasing her new debut album "Eleutheromaniac"; she also released "Happy Neon" and "301 to Paradise" to digital retailers independently in the same month.
She premiered the lead single of "Eleutheromaniac", "Yard Sale", in August 2014.
In January 2015, Neon released "Sparks" as the first single from the album.
In March 2015, Hitch released the EP "24:00" for free.
In 2016, it was announced that Hitch had changed the name of her debut album to "Anarchy", which was released on July 22, 2016.
The lead single from the album, "Please", was released on July 8, 2016.
The album did not contain any of the singles intended to be on "Eluetheromaniac", but included a promomotional single she released in 2015, "Freedom".
"The Love Song" is the title of a debut song co-written and recorded by American country music Jeff Bates.
It was released in December 2002 as the lead-off single from his debut album "Rainbow Man", released on RCA Nashville.
The song was also Bates' highest-charting single on the Hot Country Singles & Tracks (now Hot Country Songs) chart, reaching a peak of #8.
Bates wrote this song with Kenny Beard and Casey Beathard.
"Crazy Over You" is a debut song recorded by American country music duo Foster & Lloyd, who also wrote the song.
It was released in May 1987 as the first single from their self-titled debut album.
It was their most successful single, peaking at #4 on the "Billboard" Hot Country Songs chart in 1987.
The song was simultaneously recorded and released by Ricky Van Shelton on his debut album, "Wild-Eyed Dream".
"A Little More Country Than That" is a debut song written by Joey + Rory's Rory Lee Feek, Wynn Varble and Don Poythress, and recorded by American country music artist Easton Corbin.
It was released in August 2009 as the first single from his self-titled debut album.
In April 2010, the song reached Number One on the country music charts, making Corbin the first male solo artist in seven years to send a debut single to Number One.
It has been certified Gold by the RIAA.
Malinda Gayle "Mindy" McCready (November 30, 1975 – February 17, 2013) was an American country music singer.
Active from 1995 until her death in 2013, she recorded a total of five studio albums.
Her debut album, 1996's "Ten Thousand Angels", was released on BNA Records and was certified 2× Platinum by the RIAA, while 1997's "If I Don't Stay the Night" was certified Gold.
1999's "I'm Not So Tough", her final album for BNA, was less successful, and she left the label.
A self-titled fourth album followed in 2002 on Capitol Records.
McCready's fifth and final studio album, "I'm Still Here", was released in March 2010 on Iconic Records.
"You Won't Ever Be Lonely" is a debut song co-written and recorded by American country music singer Andy Griggs.
It was released in November 1998 as his debut single, and was served as the lead-off single and title track from his debut album "You Won't Ever Be Lonely".
It peaked at number 2 on the "Billboard" Hot Country Singles & Tracks charts.
Griggs wrote the song with Brett Jones.
"Ten Thousand Angels" is a debut song written by Billy Henderson and Steven Dale Jones, and recorded by American country music artist Mindy McCready.
It was released on April 1, 1996 as the first single and title track from the album "Ten Thousand Angels".
The song reached #6 on the "Billboard" Hot Country Singles & Tracks chart.
"Drunker Than Me" is the title of a debut song co-written and recorded by American country music artist Trent Tomlinson.
It was released in October 2005 as the first single from his debut album "Country Is My Rock".
Tomlinson wrote the song with Ashe Underwood.
"A Better Man" is title of a debut song co-written and performed by American country music artist Clint Black.
It was released in February 1989 as his debut single, and was served as the first single from his debut album, "Killin' Time".
It was written by Black and Hayden Nicholas.

When "A Better Man" went to No. 1 on the "Billboard magazine" Hot Country Singles chart on June 10, Black was the first artist since Freddy Fender to ascend to the top of the country chart with his first charted single.
In addition, "A Better Man" was the No. 1 song of 1989 on the Hot Country Singles chart.
The success of "A Better Man," along with the follow-up "Killin' Time," were instrumental in Black winning the Country Music Association's Horizon Award in 1989.
"What I Really Meant to Say" is a debut song written by Cyndi Thomson, Tommy Lee James and Chris Waters.
It was released in March 2001 as Thomson's debut single, as well as the lead-off single from her debut album "My World".
The song reached the top of the Hot Country Singles & Tracks (now Hot Country Songs) chart, giving Thomson her only Number One single on that chart.
It also made her the third country music artist that year to reach Number One with a debut single.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question` and `passages`, produce the single field `summary`.

Goal: compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a short intermediate state that makes it easy for the next-hop agent to (a) see what facts are present per candidate/entity, (b) see what is ambiguous or missing, and (c) know 1–4 prioritized next-hop retrieval targets or concise queries. This module does not produce the final answer unless the provided passages already unambiguously contain every fact required to resolve the question's relation for every argument.

Procedure:
1) Decide whether the question's relation is semantically complete given the supplied passages. Treat the hop as INCOMPLETE if any of the following hold: a) the relation requires combining or comparing facts across pages (e.g., comparing birth dates, publication/founding years, ordinal ranks, confirming a named director among candidates) and the passages do not supply each required fact unambiguously for all relevant arguments; b) any relation argument is ambiguous or has multiple candidate pages or mentions in the passages; c) an expected candidate page or explicit fact for an argument is missing (e.g., only one side of a comparison is present). Do not trigger INCOMPLETE solely from surface tokens (years, digits) — base the decision on whether the relation's arguments are unambiguously resolved.

2) If the hop is COMPLETE (all required facts to resolve the relation for every argument are present unambiguously in the passages): produce a concise derived result in `summary`. This may be a short phrase or sentence that states the necessary fact(s) needed by the pipeline. Keep it minimal and grounded in the passages; you may include a 1–2 token citation (passage title) if helpful.

3) If the hop is INCOMPLETE or AMBIGUOUS: do NOT assert a final answer or perform the final comparison/selection. Instead emit a short structured report in `summary` containing these labeled elements (prefer bullets or a compact paragraph with explicit labels). Keep all text neutral and non-assertive.

  - evidence: For each named candidate/argument mentioned in the question or passages, list the candidate label and the exact passage title(s) that mention it plus a very short quoted snippet or explicit fact token found there (1–12 words). If a candidate is not mentioned, say so briefly.

  - ambiguities_or_missing: Explicitly name any ambiguities (e.g., multiple candidates for the same name, multiple pages that could be the intended entity) and any missing expected pages or facts.

  - missing_facts_or_bridge: State exactly which fact(s) or disambiguation is needed to complete the relation (e.g., "birth date for Person A", "publication year of Work B", "director credit for Film C"). Be as specific and minimal as possible.

  - retrieval_direction: Provide 1–4 prioritized next-hop targets or concise queries to resolve the missing facts/ambiguities. Prefer explicit page titles when available (exact if known), otherwise provide short search-style queries (few words) or a clear description of the document to retrieve. Order targets by priority.

Formatting and style constraints (apply to both COMPLETE and INCOMPLETE cases):
- The output must be a single `summary` field only. Do not emit other fields.
- In INCOMPLETE reports, use neutral, non-assertive phrasing. Do not produce final comparisons, numeric answers, or definitive selections (e.g., avoid "X is the oldest" or "Answer: 3").
- Keep the report compact and actionable (short labeled bullets or one concise paragraph). Avoid long prose.
- It is allowed and encouraged to quote short factual snippets from passages and cite passage titles to show grounding.
- Do not defer or mark INCOMPLETE when the passages already unambiguously provide all facts for the relation and its arguments.

Produce the `summary` content that follows these rules.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the single field `query`.

Goal
- Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-intent query suitable for a second-hop search that will recover the missing bridging evidence needed to answer the question. Do not produce the final answer in `query`.

Semantic decision rule (when to emit a retrieval-intent query)
- If summary_1 does NOT assert a confirmed linking entity or verified bridging fact needed to answer the question, you MUST emit exactly one compact retrieval-intent query. "Does not assert a confirmed bridge" includes short or candidate-only summaries (e.g., a single named entity, a quoted candidate title, or numeric anchors that are not verified) and any case where the visible anchors do not unambiguously identify the needed bridge.
- If summary_1 already names a confirmed bridge (a clearly identified, verified linking entity or unambiguous metadata that directly connects the question's pieces), you may produce a compact query to fetch supporting evidence, but do NOT produce an answer in the `query` field.
- If summary_1 explicitly states that the bridge cannot be recovered automatically (extreme ambiguity or user-local context requiring clarification), do NOT force a misleading retrieval query. In that case, set `query` to the single short token BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE (all caps, no punctuation) so downstream steps can prompt the user for clarification. Do not invent facts.

Query construction rules
- Output exactly one compact line of text as `query`. Keep it short and retrieval-focused (few keywords, no full sentences). Use visible anchors from `question` and `summary_1` and minimal retrieval keywords that express the missing relation (e.g., anchor1 anchor2 which artist recorded both; AlbumTitle album artist genre; 1991–92 NHL season regular season).
- Do not include the final answer, assertions, or user-facing instructions in the `query` field. Avoid polite phrasing, questions to the user, or explanatory text. Prefer noun phrases and relation keywords.
- If your natural impulse would be to write a declarative answer or a clarification sentence in `query`, instead convert that output into a compact retrieval-intent query that targets the missing bridge.
- Preserve semantic fidelity: base the query only on visible anchors and relations in `question` and `summary_1`. Do not invent hidden identifiers or assume unseen facts.

Format contract
- Return exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short string. If unrecoverable as described above, set `query` to BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE.

Examples of disallowed outputs for `query`
- A declarative answer (e.g., "The artist was X.")
- A user-facing clarification sentence (e.g., "Do you mean X or Y?") unless summary_1 explicitly requires clarification — in that case use BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE.

Behavioral constraints
- Do not use surface heuristics (token count, punctuation, or quotation marks) as the trigger; apply the semantic rule above.
- Emit only one compact retrieval string when the bridge is missing; do not emit multiple queries or long explanations.

Inputs available: `question`, `summary_1`. Output required: single field `query`.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`. The `summary` must be a concise, post-retrieval integration of the evidence that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Follow these rules when composing `summary`:

- Purpose: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` into a grounded state that retains or resolves the earlier bridge only when the second-hop passages supply relation- or attribute-facts that are necessary to answer or disambiguate the `question` (for example: partnership/membership links, joint-award partners indicated by 'with', or requested biographical qualifiers such as nationality, birthdate, current team/status).

- When to append supporting sentence(s): Only append short passage-grounded supporting sentence(s) when the question's semantic intent requires relation or attribute facts to answer or disambiguate. In those cases, produce a compact integrated summary that (a) preserves the concise primary answer or canonical label/token chosen from `context`, and (b) appends one short supporting sentence per relevant entity (or one joint sentence for explicit partnerships) that is directly supported by the supplied `passages` and identity-aligned to the entity referenced in `context`.

- When not to append: Do not append supporting sentences and instead return only the canonical label/token when any of the following apply: the question expects a normalized identifier or single token (examples: binary/multiple-choice label selection between named options, identification-only questions, or explicit token-extraction such as 'middle name'); or the `context` already contains a full, passage-consistent, multi-clause integrated answer. These are semantic exceptions — do not append merely because an answer in `context` is short.

- Identity alignment and verification: Before appending, confirm that the fact in `passages` refers to the same entity referenced in `context` (match names, common aliases, roles, dates, or other corroborating attributes). If identity is ambiguous or unsupported, refrain from appending and preserve uncertainty instead.

- Style and content constraints: Keep output concise. If appending, limit `summary` to 1–3 sentences total (the primary concise answer plus at most one short supporting sentence per relevant entity or one joint supporting sentence). Avoid inventing facts, avoid long verbatim quotations from `passages`, and avoid name-collision errors. Do not change which label or name is selected by the existing answer-selection logic.

- If there is insufficient information in `context` and `passages` to resolve the needed relation/attribute, summarize the uncertainty succinctly rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Produce only the `summary` field as the integrated output string.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use only summary_1 and summary_2 as evidence. Treat summary_2 as the later, integrated evidence and prioritize it when reconciling information; use summary_1 only as the earlier, pre-second-hop context. Do not assume the final state is unresolved solely because summary_1 contains bridging or retrieval-oriented language.

Produce exactly one field, answer, whose value is the minimal final answer string that is fully supported by the visible summaries. The answer field must contain only that minimal plain-text string and nothing else (no explanation, no surrounding quotes, no JSON wrappers, no debugging or transport artifacts).

Decide whether the question's intent is binary (a truth-value / yes-no question) or non-binary (asking for a name, title, date, place, numeric count, selection, or other factual value). Make this decision semantically (based on the question's meaning), not by surface heuristics like token counts or punctuation.

- If the question's intent is binary AND both summary_1 and summary_2 unambiguously agree on the same truth-value, emit exactly one lowercase token: either yes or no (and nothing else) as the answer value.

- For all other question intents (non-binary), emit the minimal factual string supported by the summaries exactly as needed: preserve multi-token values, necessary punctuation, full date formats, and place formats (for example, full names, "City, State", or "Month Day, Year"), and do not truncate these to a single token.

If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not jointly support an unambiguous minimal answer, do not fabricate or over-assert; follow the system's established fallback behavior for ambiguous or unsupported questions.

Always forbid emitting serialized queries, JSON/dict-like structures, internal-debug wrappers, or other transport artifacts as the final answer. The answer must be plain text only.

Make all judgments about minimality and agreement based on the semantic content of summary_1 and summary_2, with priority to summary_2 when they differ.

Produce the answer value exactly as specified above in the answer field.

response.question: Who wrote the debut song on the debut album of the artist who also released the album "I'm Still Here"?
response.gold_answer: Billy Henderson and Steven Dale Jones

response.hop1_query: Who wrote the debut song on the debut album of the artist who also released the album "I'm Still Here"?
hop1_docs: Cold (But I'm Still Here) | "Cold (But I'm Still Here)" is the first single off Evans Blue's debut album, "The Melody and the Energetic Nature of Volume".  The song was released December 13, 2005, two months prior to the album release, and garnered frequent radio play in anticipation of the upcoming album.  Evans Blue's official MySpace explains the meaning of this song as well as others by the group.  A music video was also produced for "Cold (But I'm Still Here)."  The song currently has over three million plays on MySpace.

Kick Back Relax | "Kick Back Relax" is a pop song recorded by Swedish singer and Idol winner Agnes taken from her second album "Stronger".  The track was written by Jörgen Elofsson who also wrote her debut single "Right Here Right Now".  It was released as the album's first single in September 2006.

Crazy Over You | "Crazy Over You" is a debut song recorded by American country music duo Foster & Lloyd, who also wrote the song.  It was released in May 1987 as the first single from their self-titled debut album.  It was their most successful single, peaking at #4 on the "Billboard" Hot Country Songs chart in 1987.  The song was simultaneously recorded and released by Ricky Van Shelton on his debut album, "Wild-Eyed Dream".

Outta Here (song) | "Outta Here" is the title song of the debut studio album by Dutch R&B singer-songwriter Esmée Denters.  It was released as the debut single both from the artist and the album in the Netherlands on 14 April 2009, 27 April in New Zealand followed by 16 August in the UK.

Gabriela (singer) | Her self-titled debut album was released in 2001; it was produced by Vehnee Saturno, who also wrote eight of the album's ten songs.  Another of the songs was a rendition of Bryan Adams's "Please Forgive Me".  Her second album, "In the Interim", was released in 2004.

Anywhere but Here (song) | "Anywhere but Here" is the title of the fourth single released by American country music artist Sammy Kershaw.  It was co-written by Bob DiPiero and John Scott Sherrill with Buddy Cannon, who also produced it.  It was released in September 1992 as the final single from his debut album, "Don't Go Near the Water".  It peaked at #10 on the "Billboard" Hot Country Songs chart and #17 on the Canadian "RPM" country singles chart.

Dollhouse (Melanie Martinez song) | "Dollhouse" is the debut single by American recording artist Melanie Martinez.  The song was featured on Martinez's debut EP "Dollhouse" (2014), where it was released as the EP's lead single on February 9, 2014.  "Dollhouse" was later featured on Martinez's debut studio album, "Cry Baby" (2015).  Lyrically, "Dollhouse" is about a dysfunctional family, who, according to Martinez, is "hiding being a perfect plastic facade".  Martinez has also stated that "Dollhouse" is a metaphor for how people view celebrities and their seemingly perfect public lives.  Dollhouse is also the second song of Cry Baby, and this song is also the first official song Melanie Martinez wrote.
response.summary_1:
- evidence:
  - Evans Blue — passage [1] "Cold (But I'm Still Here)" is "the first single off Evans Blue's debut album" (snippet: "first single off Evans Blue's debut album").
  - Melanie Martinez — passage [7] "'Dollhouse' is the debut single" and "this song is also the first official song Melanie Martinez wrote." (snippets: "debut single"; "first official song Melanie Martinez wrote")
  - Foster & Lloyd — passage [3] "'Crazy Over You' is a debut song ... who also wrote the song." (snippet: "who also wrote the song")
  - Esmée Denters — passage [4] "'Outta Here' is the title song of the debut studio album" (snippet: "title song of the debut studio album")
  - Agnes — passage [2] notes songwriter Jörgen Elofsson wrote her "debut single 'Right Here Right Now'." (snippet: "wrote her debut single 'Right Here Right Now'")
  - Sammy Kershaw — passage [6] "'Anywhere but Here' ... final single from his debut album" and lists co-writers Bob DiPiero, John Scott Sherrill, Buddy Cannon (snippet: "co-written by Bob DiPiero and John Scott Sherrill with Buddy Cannon")
  - Gabriela (singer) — passage [5] debut album "produced by Vehnee Saturno, who also wrote eight of the album's ten songs." (snippet: "wrote eight of the album's ten songs")
  - Note: None of the passages explicitly mention an album titled "I'm Still Here" or state which artist released an album with that exact title.

- ambiguities_or_missing:
  - The target artist ("the artist who also released the album 'I'm Still Here'") is not identified in the provided passages.
  - It's ambiguous whether "I'm Still Here" refers to an album title, a song title, or an album by any of the artists listed in the passages.
  - For candidates listed above, songwriter credit for the debut album's opening/debut track is missing or not fully specified in several passages (e.g., Evans Blue, Esmée Denters, Gabriela).

- missing_facts_or_bridge:
  - Identify which artist released an album titled "I'm Still Here" (artist name and page).
  - For that artist: identify their debut album and the debut song (track #1 or the album's lead/debut single).
  - Obtain the songwriting credit(s) for that debut song.

- retrieval_direction (prioritized):
  1. Search/query: "I'm Still Here (album) Wikipedia" or exact page "I'm Still Here (album)".
  2. If no exact page: search "album 'I'm Still Here' artist" or "I'm Still Here album who released".
  3. Once artist page found: retrieve that artist's discography or debut album page (e.g., "Artist name discography" or "Artist name debut album").
  4. Retrieve the debut album's track listing or the specific debut song page to get the songwriter credit (query: "Artist - [debut album name] track listing" or "[song title] songwriters").
response.hop2_query: Im Still Here album artist debut single songwriter
hop2_docs: Outta Here (song) | "Outta Here" is the title song of the debut studio album by Dutch R&B singer-songwriter Esmée Denters.  It was released as the debut single both from the artist and the album in the Netherlands on 14 April 2009, 27 April in New Zealand followed by 16 August in the UK.

I'm Still Here (album) | I'm Still Here is the fifth and final studio album by American country music artist Mindy McCready.  It was released on March 23, 2010, via Linus Entertainment.  The album includes the single "I'm Still Here," as well as re-recordings of her 1996 singles "Guys Do It All the Time" and "Ten Thousand Angels."  Mindy performed on "Fox & Friends" to promote the album in April 2010.  This is McCready's final release before her death in 2013.

Dami Im discography | The discography of South Korean-born Australian recording artist Dami Im consists of three studio albums, two extended plays, ten singles, two album appearances, and four music videos. Im began her music career as a gospel singer in Korea and independently released her debut studio album, "Dream", in 2010.  She was the winner on the fifth season of "The X Factor Australia" in 2013, and subsequently received a contract with Sony Music Australia.  Im released her self-titled second studio album in November 2013, which features selected songs she performed as part of the top twelve on "The X Factor".  The album debuted at number one on the ARIA Albums Chart and was certified platinum by the Australian Recording Industry Association (ARIA), denoting shipments of 70,000 copies.  Additionally, the album also included Im's debut single "Alive", which topped the ARIA Singles Chart and was certified platinum.  She became the first "X Factor Australia" contestant to follow up a number one single with a number one album on the ARIA Charts.

Here with Me (Dido song) | "Here with Me" is the debut single by English singer-songwriter Dido.  It was the first single she released from her 1999 debut studio album, "No Angel."  The song was written about her then-boyfriend Bob Page.

Heart Beats (Dami Im album) | Heart Beats is the second studio album by Australian recording artist Dami Im, released on 14 October 2014 by Sony Music Australia.  While Im's previous self-titled debut album consisted of cover songs she performed on "The X Factor Australia", "Heart Beats" features a mixture of original ballads and up-tempo pop songs.  Im worked on the album with many songwriters and producers, including Robby De Sa, Jon Hume, Christian Lo Russo, Louis Schoorl, DNA Songs, Trinity and Hayley Warner.

Cold (But I'm Still Here) | "Cold (But I'm Still Here)" is the first single off Evans Blue's debut album, "The Melody and the Energetic Nature of Volume".  The song was released December 13, 2005, two months prior to the album release, and garnered frequent radio play in anticipation of the upcoming album.  Evans Blue's official MySpace explains the meaning of this song as well as others by the group.  A music video was also produced for "Cold (But I'm Still Here)."  The song currently has over three million plays on MySpace.

Right Here (Boh Runga album) | Right Here is the debut solo album of New Zealand singer/songwriter Boh Runga, released in May, 2009.  "Right Here" was recorded in Marshall Altman's Hollywood studio and includes the single "Evelyn".
